understanding: This is a single self-report survey ('AP1and2_April_25.csv') completed by 9 social enterprises in the Aurelia/YSB (FFA) accelerator cohort, all working on health, hygiene and WASH services. Each row is one enterprise and captures (a) accelerator-experience and business-maturity self-ratings, (b) a five-stage people-reach funnel — Inform → Engage → Outcomes → Impact → Societal Impact — each with counts, a women-share figure, a description of the group reached, and a note on how the number was calculated, and (c) basic financials (funding, revenue, runway, employees, customers). Most funnel questions appear TWICE: an 'as of October 2024' version and a later undated version; the undated versions (f12, f18, f24, f30, f36) are complete (0% null) while the October versions are 22–44% null, so the undated set is the more usable, more recent data. The file can tell you roughly how many people the cohort reaches and how far they move down the funnel, but it cannot verify those figures: they are self-reported, mix direct and indirect beneficiaries, several enterprises repeat the same number across every stage, and cells contain messy tokens ('800 agents', 'N/A', 'Too early to obtain data', '10,000').
assessment: Across 9 enterprises in this cohort, the self-reported numbers suggest health and hygiene messaging reached about 42,000 people, with roughly 26,000 directly engaged. But the funnel doesn't hold together — the stages that should get smaller actually grow, ending with a societal-impact claim of about 72,000 people (bigger than the 42,000 first reached), so these figures can't yet be trusted as a real reach-to-lasting-change story.

insights.text: The funnel runs backwards: 42,000 informed narrows to 26,000 engaged, then swells again to 29,000 at behaviour-change and 72,000 at societal impact. Later stages should be smaller than earlier ones, so the 70% "first-contact-to-behaviour-change" rate is not reliable — treat it as a data error to fix, not a result to celebrate.

At least one enterprise (EverGreen Access) reports 700 people changing behaviour while only 27 were engaged and 0 informed — impossible on its face. Go back to that enterprise and any others whose later-stage numbers exceed their earlier ones before quoting any totals.

These are self-reports from a single 9-enterprise survey, mixing direct and indirect beneficiaries and different counting methods (head counts, attendance, "800 agents", "50 Schools"). Several enterprises repeat the same number across every stage, so the totals likely double-count people and can't confirm genuine step-by-step progress.

There is no comparison group and only a self-scored accelerator rating, so we cannot say the programme caused these changes. If you need to show impact, prioritise cleaning the messy cells and agreeing one clear counting method per stage for the next round.

plan.understanding: This is a single self-report survey ('AP1and2_April_25.csv') completed by 9 social enterprises in the Aurelia/YSB (FFA) accelerator cohort, all working on health, hygiene and WASH services. Each row is one enterprise and captures (a) accelerator-experience and business-maturity self-ratings, (b) a five-stage people-reach funnel — Inform → Engage → Outcomes → Impact → Societal Impact — each with counts, a women-share figure, a description of the group reached, and a note on how the number was calculated, and (c) basic financials (funding, revenue, runway, employees, customers). Most funnel questions appear TWICE: an 'as of October 2024' version and a later undated version; the undated versions (f12, f18, f24, f30, f36) are complete (0% null) while the October versions are 22–44% null, so the undated set is the more usable, more recent data. The file can tell you roughly how many people the cohort reaches and how far they move down the funnel, but it cannot verify those figures: they are self-reported, mix direct and indirect beneficiaries, several enterprises repeat the same number across every stage, and cells contain messy tokens ('800 agents', 'N/A', 'Too early to obtain data', '10,000').

tables.rationale: One row per enterprise with self-reported answers to a structured questionnaire covering reach counts, beneficiary descriptions, accelerator feedback and financials — a classic survey response table, not a transactional log or ledger.

proposedMetrics.name: People reached with health & hygiene messaging (Inform)
proposedMetrics.description: Total number of people who received educational health & hygiene messaging through the enterprises' brands — the top of the reach funnel.
proposedMetrics.howCalculated: Adds up the answers to the undated 'Inform: How many people have received educational health & hygiene messaging...' column (f12) across all 9 enterprises. The undated column is used instead of the 'as of October 2024' one (f11) because it has no blanks and is more recent.
assumptions: Used f12 (undated, 0% null) rather than f11 (33% null, 'as of October 2024') as the more complete and more recent version of the same question.
Counts are self-reported totals combining direct and indirect beneficiaries, per the calculation notes in f15.
caveats: Figures are unverified self-reports and mix direct and indirect reach.
Cells include formatted tokens (e.g. '10,000', '15000.0') that require cell-by-cell normalization.
Summing across enterprises may double-count people reached by more than one organisation.

proposedMetrics.name: People directly engaged (two-way interaction)
proposedMetrics.description: Total number of people directly engaged in a two-way interaction, e.g. actively participating in a handwashing training or workshop.
proposedMetrics.howCalculated: Adds up the answers to the undated 'Engage: How many people have been directly engaged in a two-way interaction...' column (f18) across all 9 enterprises. The undated column is used instead of the messy 'as of May 2025' version (f17, which contains entries like '800 agents' and 'N/A').
assumptions: Used f18 (undated, clean numeric) rather than f17 (mixed types, 33% null).
'Engaged' is interpreted as active two-way participation per the question wording.
caveats: Self-reported and unverified.
Definitions of 'engagement' vary between enterprises (some report agents onboarded rather than trainees).
Several enterprises repeat the same figure across funnel stages, so engage may equal inform for them.

proposedMetrics.name: People experiencing behaviour change / improved access (Impact)
proposedMetrics.description: Total number of people who experience positive day-to-day changes such as washing hands more frequently or gaining improved access to health and hygiene services — the enterprise-level 'real, lasting improvement' the goal asks about.
proposedMetrics.howCalculated: Adds up the answers to the undated 'Impact: Number of people that experience positive changes in their day-to-day lives...' column (f30) across all 9 enterprises, chosen over the 'as of October 2024' version (f29) because it has no blanks.
assumptions: Used f30 (undated, 0% null) over f29 (33% null).
Treated behaviour change / improved access (Impact stage) as the closest proxy for 'changed behaviour' in the project goal.
caveats: Behaviour change is self-reported with no baseline or verification method described.
Some enterprises report identical numbers for outcomes and impact, suggesting the distinction was not consistently applied.
No follow-up over time, so 'lasting' cannot actually be evidenced.

proposedMetrics.name: First-contact-to-behaviour-change rate
proposedMetrics.description: Share of people reached with messaging who go on to experience a positive behaviour change or improved access — a funnel conversion answering 'of those we reach, how many actually change'.
proposedMetrics.howCalculated: Divides the total in the Impact column (f30) by the total in the Inform column (f12) across all 9 enterprises, giving the proportion of reached people who report a lasting change.
assumptions: Inform (f12) is the funnel entry point and Impact (f30) the deepest reliably-populated stage.
Both counts are drawn from the same undated column set for consistency.
caveats: Numerator and denominator are independent self-reports, not the same tracked cohort, so this is an aggregate ratio not a true individual conversion rate.

Enterprises reporting identical figures at both stages inflate the ratio toward 1; others report impact figures that exceed inform figures (e.g. undated impact for some rows), which can push the ratio above 100%.
Highly sensitive to a few large values.

stages.label: Engage — directly engaged in two-way interaction
Societal Impact — material improvement in health/quality of life

orderedFunnel.rationale: The questionnaire is explicitly built as a numbered reach ladder (1. Inform → 2. Engage → 3. Outcomes → 4. Impact → 5. Societal Impact) within the same table, each stage a headcount of people moving deeper from first contact to lasting change. The undated column of each pair is used because it is fully populated and more recent.

activity.rationale: Enterprises describe the concrete activities they ran to reach people — school outreaches, WASHathon and Germ Blast programmes, ECD forums, community activations, agent networks and radio marketing (f16, f21) — and how counts were compiled (f15).

output.rationale: The Inform (f12) and Engage (f18) headcounts are direct outputs — the number of people reached with messaging and directly engaged in trainings/workshops.

outcome.rationale: The Outcomes stage (f24) counts people with immediate knowledge/awareness gains (e.g. learning to wash hands), and the Impact stage (f30) counts those changing behaviour or gaining improved access — short-term changes in people's lives.

impact.rationale: The Societal Impact column (f36) captures material longer-term change (ill less often, higher income), but it is the least reliably answered (several 'Too early to obtain data' and 0 entries) and has no time-series to demonstrate durability.

what.rationale: What changed: people gained health & hygiene knowledge and awareness ('Outcomes', f24), started washing hands more often or got better access to hygiene services ('Impact', f30), and for some a material improvement in health or income ('Societal Impact', f36).

who.rationale: Who was affected: mostly children from low-income township and public-school households, women (including women-owned ECDs), youth and people with disabilities in underserved communities in Nigeria, Brazil and South Africa — described in the 'vulnerable and marginalised groups' columns (f13, f25) with a women-share figure (f14).

howMuch.rationale: How big: the cohort reports reaching thousands per enterprise at the Inform stage (f12, up to 15,000) narrowing through Engage (f18) and Impact (f30), with the largest societal-impact claim at 54,000 (f36). Scale is quantified per stage, though depth and duration are not tracked over time.

contribution.rationale: Would it have happened anyway: only a self-assessed accelerator-attribution score (f2, 1–10) and an 'influence on business performance' rating (f3) speak to contribution — there is no comparison group or counterfactual, so attribution of the beneficiary changes to the programme cannot be established.

risk.rationale: How solid is the evidence / what could go wrong: no field assesses impact risk, evidence quality or drop-off. The only hints are messy self-report artefacts ('Too early to obtain data', repeated identical numbers across stages), which are data-quality warnings rather than a structured risk dimension.

frameworkTags.label: SDG 6 — Clean Water and Sanitation (candidate)
frameworkTags.rationale: The funnel is explicitly about health, hygiene, handwashing, WASH and sanitation access (f16, f24, f30), which aligns with SDG 6 themes.
frameworkTags.caveat: Candidate interpretation only, inferred from question wording; not a verified or official SDG mapping.

frameworkTags.label: SDG 3 — Good Health and Well-being (candidate)
frameworkTags.rationale: Societal Impact tracks people who are 'ill less often' and experience improved health/quality of life (f36), consistent with SDG 3.
frameworkTags.caveat: Candidate only; based on self-reported outcome descriptions, not measured health data.

frameworkTags.label: Impact Management Project — What, Who, How Much (candidate)
frameworkTags.rationale: The dataset provides clear What (knowledge and behaviour change), Who (vulnerable groups, women share) and How Much (staged headcounts) evidence, with weaker Contribution and no Risk dimension.
frameworkTags.caveat: Candidate framing of the fields against the Five Dimensions; not a certified IMP assessment.

frameworkTags.label: IRIS+ client/individuals reached themes (candidate)
frameworkTags.rationale: People-reached and beneficiary-demographic counts resemble IRIS+ style output/outcome metrics on individuals served and stakeholder demographics.
frameworkTags.caveat: Candidate resemblance only; no official IRIS+ metric codes were supplied and none are asserted.

uncertainties: Each funnel question appears twice ('as of October 2024' vs undated); the undated columns (f12, f18, f24, f30, f36) were chosen as fresher and complete, but which period the enterprise intended is not certain.
Several enterprises report the same number across multiple stages (e.g. 15000, 1000, 700, 7800), so the funnel may not represent genuine stage-by-stage filtering, undermining conversion ratios.
Some impact/outcome figures exceed the inform figure for the same enterprise, so aggregate ratios can exceed 100% and stage ordering is not internally consistent everywhere.
Counts mix direct and indirect beneficiaries and use different calculation bases (head counts, attendance, agents onboarded, households) per the calculation-note columns.

Messy cells present: currency tokens ('$19500.00'), thousands separators ('10,000', '64,000'), unit text ('800 agents', '50 Schools'), and non-numeric entries ('N/A', 'Too early to obtain data') needing normalization.
Women-share columns (f14, f20, f26, f32, f38) inconsistently mix percentages (50, 75) and absolute counts (1000, 810), so they were not used in KPIs.
Only 9 rows (one cohort), so totals are small-sample and heavily influenced by one or two large self-reports.
No unique beneficiary identifier and only one table, so no join or de-duplication across enterprises is possible; summed totals may double-count individuals.
Attribution to the accelerator relies solely on subjective scores (f2, f3) with no counterfactual, and there is no risk/evidence-quality field.

definition.name: People reached with health & hygiene messaging (Inform)
definition.description: Total number of people who received educational health & hygiene messaging through the enterprises' brands — the top of the reach funnel.
definition.howCalculated: Adds up the answers to the undated 'Inform: How many people have received educational health & hygiene messaging...' column (f12) across all 9 enterprises. The undated column is used instead of the 'as of October 2024' one (f11) because it has no blanks and is more recent.
assumptions: Used f12 (undated, 0% null) rather than f11 (33% null, 'as of October 2024') as the more complete and more recent version of the same question.
Counts are self-reported totals combining direct and indirect beneficiaries, per the calculation notes in f15.
caveats: Figures are unverified self-reports and mix direct and indirect reach.
Cells include formatted tokens (e.g. '10,000', '15000.0') that require cell-by-cell normalization.
Summing across enterprises may double-count people reached by more than one organisation.

evidence.formula: We added up the number of people who received health & hygiene messaging (the "Inform" column) across all 9 organisations, giving 42,269 people reached.
caveats: All values were stored as decimals like "15000.0" and read as whole people.
EverGreen Access reported 0 in the "Inform" column, which was kept as a real zero.

definition.name: People directly engaged (two-way interaction)
definition.description: Total number of people directly engaged in a two-way interaction, e.g. actively participating in a handwashing training or workshop.
definition.howCalculated: Adds up the answers to the undated 'Engage: How many people have been directly engaged in a two-way interaction...' column (f18) across all 9 enterprises. The undated column is used instead of the messy 'as of May 2025' version (f17, which contains entries like '800 agents' and 'N/A').
assumptions: Used f18 (undated, clean numeric) rather than f17 (mixed types, 33% null).
'Engaged' is interpreted as active two-way participation per the question wording.
caveats: Self-reported and unverified.
Definitions of 'engagement' vary between enterprises (some report agents onboarded rather than trainees).
Several enterprises repeat the same figure across funnel stages, so engage may equal inform for them.

evidence.formula: We added up the number of people directly engaged in a two-way interaction (the "Engage" column) across all 9 organisations, giving 26,496 people.
caveats: EcoPulse Technologies and ThriveWater Group reported 0 engaged people, kept as real zeros.

definition.name: People experiencing behaviour change / improved access (Impact)
definition.description: Total number of people who experience positive day-to-day changes such as washing hands more frequently or gaining improved access to health and hygiene services — the enterprise-level 'real, lasting improvement' the goal asks about.
definition.howCalculated: Adds up the answers to the undated 'Impact: Number of people that experience positive changes in their day-to-day lives...' column (f30) across all 9 enterprises, chosen over the 'as of October 2024' version (f29) because it has no blanks.
assumptions: Used f30 (undated, 0% null) over f29 (33% null).
Treated behaviour change / improved access (Impact stage) as the closest proxy for 'changed behaviour' in the project goal.
caveats: Behaviour change is self-reported with no baseline or verification method described.
Some enterprises report identical numbers for outcomes and impact, suggesting the distinction was not consistently applied.
No follow-up over time, so 'lasting' cannot actually be evidenced.

evidence.formula: We added up the number of people who experienced positive behaviour change or improved access (the "Impact" column) across all 9 organisations, giving 29,589 people.
caveats: EverGreen Access reports 700 people changing behaviour while only 27 were engaged and 0 informed — the later-stage number is larger than the earlier stages, which is worth checking.
ThriveWater Group reported 0, kept as a real zero.

definition.name: First-contact-to-behaviour-change rate
definition.description: Share of people reached with messaging who go on to experience a positive behaviour change or improved access — a funnel conversion answering 'of those we reach, how many actually change'.
definition.howCalculated: Divides the total in the Impact column (f30) by the total in the Inform column (f12) across all 9 enterprises, giving the proportion of reached people who report a lasting change.
assumptions: Inform (f12) is the funnel entry point and Impact (f30) the deepest reliably-populated stage.
Both counts are drawn from the same undated column set for consistency.
caveats: Numerator and denominator are independent self-reports, not the same tracked cohort, so this is an aggregate ratio not a true individual conversion rate.

Enterprises reporting identical figures at both stages inflate the ratio toward 1; others report impact figures that exceed inform figures (e.g. undated impact for some rows), which can push the ratio above 100%.
Highly sensitive to a few large values.

evidence.formula: We divided the total people showing behaviour change (29,589) by the total people first reached with messaging (42,269), meaning about 70% of those informed went on to change behaviour or gain improved access.
caveats: The ratio uses the impact total (29,589) over the inform total (42,269) = 0.70.
Some organisations report more people at the impact stage than at the inform stage (e.g. EverGreen Access), which inflates this rate and should be checked.

chart.title: Impact funnel

points.label: Engage — directly engaged in two-way interaction
Societal Impact — material improvement in health/quality of life

chart.summary: Inform starts at 42,269 people, but later stages grow instead of shrinking (Societal Impact: 72,420) — a sign of reporting errors, flagged under “Needs review”. Stage totals were normalized cell-by-cell by AI review.

warnings.message: The “2. Engage: How many people have been directly engaged in a two-way interaction by your organisation e.g. actively participating in a training or workshop on handwashing as of May 2025?” column in AP1and2_April_25.csv mixes numbers and text — some values may have been skipped.
The “2.3 How did your organisation reach the target group and what was the impact on the target group?” column in AP1and2_April_25.csv mixes numbers and text — some values may have been skipped.
The “2.4 How did you calculate the number of people you reached in this group” column in AP1and2_April_25.csv mixes numbers and text — some values may have been skipped.

The “3. Outcomes: Number of people that experience immediate positive outcomes as a result of the programme through knowledge and awareness increase (e.g. they learn how to wash their hands properly) as of October 2024” column in AP1and2_April_25.csv mixes numbers and text — some values may have been skipped.

The “5. Societal Impact: people that experience material positive impact on their health and/or quality of life (e.g. they are ill less often, they now have higher income) as of October 2024” column in AP1and2_April_25.csv mixes numbers and text — some values may have been skipped.
The “How many employees (Full time employees) does your organisation currently have?” column in AP1and2_April_25.csv is 100% empty — figures based on it rest on thin data.
The “How many customers do you currently serve?” column in AP1and2_April_25.csv mixes numbers and text — some values may have been skipped.
The “Are there any partnerships that have formed as a result of the accelerator?” column in AP1and2_April_25.csv mixes numbers and text — some values may have been skipped.

“Outcomes — immediate knowledge/awareness increase” (28,869) is larger than “Engage — directly engaged in two-way interaction” (26,496) — later funnel stages should be smaller. Likely a reporting mistake worth checking at the source.

“Impact — behaviour change / improved access” (29,589) is larger than “Outcomes — immediate knowledge/awareness increase” (28,869) — later funnel stages should be smaller. Likely a reporting mistake worth checking at the source.

“Societal Impact — material improvement in health/quality of life” (72,420) is larger than “Impact — behaviour change / improved access” (29,589) — later funnel stages should be smaller. Likely a reporting mistake worth checking at the source.

secondaryChart.title: Reach vs. lasting impact, per enterprise
secondaryChart.summary: How each enterprise converts first contact into lasting change. Where the dark bar exceeds the light one (e.g. EverGreen Access: 700 impacted but 0 informed), the numbers cannot be right as reported.
